Project name: CEMBS-PolSea (hydrodynamic model of the southern Baltic)
Institutions_clean: Institute of Oceanology Polish Academy of Sciences; Satellite Environment Control of Baltic Sea (SatBałtyk); Academic Computer Center of Gdańsk (TASK)
Head_institutions: Gdańsk University of Technology; Polish Academy of Sciences; Satellite Environment Control of Baltic Sea (SatBałtyk)
Public value framing:
The numerical ecosystem model of the Baltic Sea 3D CEMBS allows tracking time and space changes of physical and biogeochemical parameters of the marine environment. An essential element of this project is to develop numerical methods for predicting the emergence of threats to our coastal area (for example, blooms of algae, sea surface height changes, ice cover reach, and others), which will become a modern tool that can help in managing the environment. The diagnosis of the processes in this project is not only important from the scientific point of view but also allows a deeper understanding of the Baltic ecosystem functioning.

It is also of a great importance for this group of people, which plans to spend free time near our coast. Operational model allows the information flow about the state of the marine environment in real-time, and 60 hours in advance. The effects of the proposed project can become a potent tool for policymakers and local authorities, especially in emergency situations.
Technical objectives: The 3D CEMBS-PolSea hydrodynamic model of the southern Baltic was developed and tested as part of the CSI-POM project. This model determines the spatiotemporal distributions of parameters such as temperature, salinity, currents and sea level. It will allow to investigate the structure, dynamics and variability of physical processes in the marine environment of the studied area.
Funding sources: Ministry of Science and Higher Education (Poland) (support of grant No NN305 111636)

Project name: "Grey Heron" AI Squid Fishing Forecast System
Institutions_clean: Shanghai Ocean University (SOU); Cnfc Overseas Fisheries Co.
Head_institutions: Shanghai Ocean University (SOU); Cnfc Overseas Fisheries Co.
Public value framing: By accurately forecasting squid fishing grounds and providing medium- to long-term stock assessments, the system eliminates the reliance on "rule-of-thumb" methods and the haphazard nature of fishing ground relocation in squid jigging operations. Additionally, the system features built-in vessel tracking and boundary violation alerts, helping to prevent maritime disputes and improve management efficiency. It also automatically generates and publishes daily "one-map" reports detailing the operational sea area environment, vessel locations, and fishing forecasts, thereby leveraging technology to empower a green and low-carbon fishing industry.
Technical objectives: By integrating technologies such as marine remote sensing, geographic information systems, artificial intelligence, and big data, we can provide accurate five-day forecasts of fishing grounds in global pelagic squid fishing areas.

Public value framing: INSPIRE brings different technlogies and solutions together focusing on the detection, collection and prevention of litter, plastics and macroplastics in rivers and on riverbanks; Detection of the pollution characteristics, such as type and level of pollution present in terms of litter, macro-, meso- and microplastics, floating, in the water column and at the riverbanks; Collection of litter, macro and microplastics and macroplastics at the riverbank and in the river. The lifetime of litter present in the river is minimized, reducing the risk of further breakdown of items into fragments and further into microlitter and prevent them from being finally discharged to the seas and ocean; Prevention of litter, macro and microplastics to enter the river by collecting it from its waste stream before it can enter the river and by developing biodegradable alternatives for currently non-degradable polluting products, to avoid they will further be used and arrive in the river as litter.
Technical objectives: Defining a set of solutions, Each solution is composed of a multitude of the applied 20 technologies; Developing guidelines for scaling up the solutions. The possible solution packages will be extended with plug-in options to add additional technologies and actions, different from the 20 implemented in INSPIRE and offer a ‘fit-for-all’ solutions applicable and replicable in all rivers in Europe.
Data collection methods: in-situ; citizen science; desk research; remote sensing

Project name: Algal Forest Restoration in Mediterranean Sea (AFRIMED)
Institutions_clean: University Chouaib Doukkali (UCD); UN Environment World Conservation Monitoring Centre (WCMC); University of Vlora “Ismail Qemali” (UVL); Polytechnic University of Marche (UNIVPM); Zoologic Station Anton Dohrn (SZN); Faculty of Sciences of Bizerte (FSB); Hellenic Agricultural Organization-Demeter (HAO-DEMETER); EcoReach; University of Girona (UdG); Hellenic Centre for Marine Research (HCMR); University of Nice Sophia Antipolis (UNS)

Public value framing: As the deployment of blue economy sectors across Europe accelerates, it is vital that the resilience of marine and coastal ecosystems is maintained and restored in order to allow economic growth to be achieved in a sustainable manner. However, despite best efforts to conserve and sustainably manage marine ecosystems, attempts to mitigate human threats are generally proving inadequate to halt biodiversity loss and habitat degradation. Consequently, there is a need for the European Union to turn policy objectives relating to secure, safe, clean and sustainably managed oceans into actions and impacts. This is particularly true in coastal habitats, where the effects of multiple stressors are causing widespread loss of critical coastal habitats, a pattern which is projected to increase with climatic change. Macroalgal forest ecosystems play a key role in enhancing coastal primary productivity, supporting complex food webs and are integral to the delivery of a multitude of goods and services. However, the massive losses of macroalgal forests that are occurring all over the Mediterranean basin are going largely unnoticed and we do not have the tools or knowledge required to bring about change. AFRIMED seeks to bring about a step-change in this situation, by developing, implementing and promoting a protocol to effectively restore macroalgal forests (Cystoseira spp.) in the Mediterranean Sea in order to maximise the delivery of conservation, societal and economic benefits
Public values (labels): mitigate biodiversity loss; climate change adaptation; blue economy
Technical objectives: AFRIMED aims we seek to improve existing and develop new innovative restoration actions in order to restore damaged or degraded macroalgal forests (Cystoseira) to the benefit the people and industries that utilise them and provide a framework from which to up-scale the approach; Specifically, the project will: - Identifying optimal locations to undertake restorative action, now and into the future, by bringing together existing knowledge of the status and distribution of macroalgal forests and the environmental and anthropogenic context of the regions in which they are found (WP1); - undertaking novel laboratory experiments in order to optimise restoration techniques in a range of environmental and anthropogenic contexts commonly found in the region (WP2); - exploring the response of Cystoseira species to future climatic conditions and modelling future distribution patterns in order to ensure restoration actions are relevant into the future (WP2); - developing indicators, targets and monitoring frameworks to evaluate restoration success and quantify subsequent changes following restoration (WP2); - passing from the lab to the field to refine restoration methods in “real-world” conditions (WP3); - identifying methods and contextual factors that improve the efficiency and success rate of restoration and the delivery of goods and services (WP4); - determining where the knowledge and lessons generated from the project can be applied to address policy needs and aspirations (WP4); - engaging with local stakeholders to promote and facilitate replicability and transferability of know-how and generate support from local groups and industries (WP5); - disseminating actions and results to increase the awareness of these important habitats and the benefits that can be obtained from restoring them (WP5).
Funding sources: Executive Agency for Small and Medium Enterprise (EASME) and European Maritime and Fisheries fund (EMFF)

Public value framing: The overall objective of the project is to develop a virtual environment equipped with FAIR multi-disciplinary data and services to support marine and freshwater scientists and stakeholders restoring healthy oceans, seas, coastal and inland waters. The AquaINFRA virtual environment will enable the target stakeholders to store, share, access, analyse and process research data and other research digital objects from their own discipline, across research infrastructures, disciplines and national borders leveraging on EOSC and the other existing operational dataspaces (e.g., EMODnet, Copernicus Marine Service, Digital Twins, etc.).The initial level of the existing solutions for data access in marine domain are at the Technology Readiness Level (TRL) 7-8. Many services, such as Blue-Cloud data discovery and access service and NextGEOSS, have reached a maturity level, where they can already be seen as finalised end-user products in terms of usability.

Public value framing: The ASAP Platform by WaterITech & WaterWebTools is a multi-source integration platform that overlay with modeling, IoT, and weather data, and enables highly advanced forecasting and analysis. The ASAP platform operationalizes the SWAT+ catchment model and the GOTM-WET lake and lagoon model. By coupling to local weather forecasts, the platform can produce 9-day hydrological forecasts for the catchment runoff as well as forecasts for the lagoon water quality dynamics. By allowing real-time sensor data to be assimilated into the model forecasts, the ASAP Platform can comprise a digital twin of a coupled catchment-lagoon/lake system.
Technical objectives: monitoring and forecasting weather and water quality

Public value framing:
We identified four key outcomes for this action:

 1. The major and first key outcome of the CORIS project is a widely available, open access, trusted source of information and services which can be used to better understand the coastal ocean in diverse coastal regions across the world. The attributes are a system of systems which brings together existing and emerging capabilities under the umbrella of a state of the art discovery and access resource for users of all types including observers, modelers, economic and business managers and policy makers. To achieve this purpose, CORIS partners with other organizations and initiatives to improve the use of their systems, expanding the user base and helping drive interoperability across global coastal oceans.
 2. A second key outcome is the broader access to common methods (best practices) and standards that can be used in lesser developed countries and elsewhere to expand local and regional forecasts and monitoring. This will include encouragement to adopt best practices and standards that increase interoperability across the coastal ocean communities.
 3. A third key outcome is to improve access to near real-time data of different nature for nowcasting, hazardous response and mitigation. This requires close collaboration with data providers to encourage the consistent implementation of quality assurance and data/metadata protocols.
  4. A fourth outcome is the access to the next generation Data Lakes and Digital Twins of the Oceans (e.g. DITTO, TURTLE) outcomes into the CORIS system of systems. The system of systems has a built in flexibility to adapt to advances that occur during the next decade and beyond. There will be some that we cannot anticipate now, but will need to include in CORIS.
Public values (labels): stakeholder inclusion; better ocean systems data
Technical objectives: range of services, including fully open access to data, tools for managing and using data, best practices for observation, data management and user applications, as well as access to relevant documentation
Funding sources: Coast Predict; CMCC Foundation; WMO; UNEP
Data collection methods: in-situ; remote sensing; citizen science; desk research

Public value framing: Restore our Coasts will address coastal restoration and resilience holistically by delivering new solutions for ecosystem restoration and management through a multidisciplinary approach that integrates in-situ observations, use of numerical models for forecasting, economic analyses and projected social impact. The approach will inform the application of site-specific nature-based solutions such as coral reef reintroduction, seagrass and mangroves restoration. Passive restoration measures removing sources of disturbance, such as establishment of marine protected areas, will also be considered. Co-design and continuous stakeholder and user collaboration at Pilot Sites in more than 14 nations, will be an important element to ensure that solutions are co-designed to support community and coastal resilience. Project name: Coastal numerical modeling at various scales
Institutions_clean: Ensenada Center for Scientific Research and Advanced Studies (CICESE)
Public value framing: The SWASH model is a non-hydrostatic model for simulating flow and the transport of passive particles in coastal areas (see http://swash.sourceforge.net/) This code, as well as the non-hydrostatic version of XBeach (see http://oss.deltares.ni/web/xbeach/), outperforms Boussinesq-style codes because they are capable of accurately resolving the vertical distribution of velocities in turbulent flows, both on small and large scales. However, to date, it is unknown which of the two codes is better, using both physical and numerical criteria, for capturing processes in the surf zone (swash zone). Furthermore, although the X-Beach code includes a module for morphodynamics and sediment transport in the swash zone, on beaches, and in dunes, SWASH lacks such a module, and this deficiency is known to be a limitation of the SWASH code. For this reason, this project proposes the following two initial objectives. 1.- Compare the performance—from the perspective of both physical processes and numerical accuracy—of the X Beach and SWASH codes in capturing the evolution of turbulent flows in the swash zone. 2. Develop a sediment transport and morphodynamics module for SWASH, and analyze its performance in the case of breaking waves that generate turbulent flows.
Technical objectives: 1. Compare the performance—from the perspective of both physical processes and numerical performance—of the X Beach and SWASH codes to capture the evolution of turbulent flows in swash zones. 2. Develop a sediment transport and morphodynamics module for SWASH, and analyze its performance in the case of breaking waves that generate turbulent flows.
Notes: Information about the research project is displayed in pop-up windows.

Public value framing:
The COAST-HF National Observation Service aims to understand and analyze changes in the coastal environment across various time scales, ranging from the dynamics of extreme or transient events occurring at high frequencies (hourly, daily) to multi-year trends. The scientific issues at the heart of COAST-HF’s objectives—which are reflected in national and international strategies—thus fall within the themes of the impact of climate change and anthropogenic pressures in coastal regions, which remain the primary areas of human settlement:
1. High-frequency and long-term observation of these changes in the coastal environment under climatic and anthropogenic pressures;
2. Detection and understanding of extreme events (e.g., floods, storms, droughts, ecological crises). Long-term trends in the frequency of these events.
3. Support for coastal zone monitoring and management policies, the implementation of European framework directives (WFD, MSFD), and support for the implementation of regional marine conventions (OSPAR, Barcelona).

Institutions_clean: Los Alamos National Laboratory
Public value framing: CICE5.1.2 is the latest released version of the Los Alamos Sea Ice Model. It is the result of a community effort to develop a portable, efficient sea ice model that can be run coupled in a global climate model or uncoupled as a stand-alone ice model. It has been released as the sea ice component of the Community Earth System Model (CESM), a fully-coupled global climate model that provides simulations of the earths past, present and future climate states. CICE5 is supported on high- and low-resolution Greenland Pole and tripole grids, which are identical to those used by the Parallel Ocean Program (POP) ocean model. The high resolution version is best suited for simulating present-day and future climate scenarios while the low resolution option is used for paleoclimate simulations and debugging.
Technical objectives: CICE is a computationally efficient model for simulating the growth, melting, and movement of polar sea ice.

Institutions_clean: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration
Public value framing:
NOAA’s Coral Reef Watch (CRW) is a free online tool that provides a global analysis of sea surface temperature (SST) and outlooks to identify coral reefs that are at risk of bleaching. Coral reefs are very sensitive to temperature changes. When the ocean gets too warm, they begin to bleach (turn white) as the tiny creatures that live in coral reefs become stressed and leave, exposing the white skeleton.

CRW is a tremendous resource to coral reef managers, allowing them to better protect and manage the planet's aquatic resources. Coral reefs are often located in distant and remote regions, coral reef managers need satellite tools to help monitor the reefs. NCEI’s data plays a central role in supporting CRW, and the global coral reefs that this product monitors.
Technical objectives: predicting coral disease
Geographic coverage: Central Pacific; Western Pacific; Australian Coast

Institutions_clean: STIMSON Center
Public value framing: CORVI is a decision support tool for leaders who need to make smart climate investments to improve the safety and security of coastal cities and island states. CORVI organizes data and information across the land and seascape to provide decision makers with the complete risk picture they need to take action.
Technical objectives: CORVI is a decision-support tool developed by Stimson Center that compares a diverse range of ecological, economic, and social risks connected to climate change to produce a coastal city or island-state risk profile, using nearly 100 indicators across 10 risk categories. By integrating empirical data, expert surveys, and local stakeholder input through structured expert judgement, CORVI fills data gaps and provides a locally informed, data-driven assessment of climate and ocean vulnerability in sub-national settings. The resulting risk profile helps governments, investors, and other decision-makers prioritize actions, guide resilience investments, and design integrated policy solutions to build climate resilience in coastal cities and island states

Public value framing: The overarching goal of this project is to further develop the coupling between a state-of-the-art process-based community land surface/hydrological model and a 3D community ocean circulation model to better predict temperature, salinity, water level, buoyancy, sediment, and contaminant transport through the River - Estuary - Ocean Continuum with the consideration of both barotropic and baroclinic processes. Major tasks in this proposed project include the coupling between ocean and hydrological models, coupling of contaminant and sediment transport modules. The coupled model will be applied to the testbed of Galveston Bay during Hurricane Harvey with a focus on how temperature, salinity and sediment concentration impact buoyancy and contaminant transport. Additionally, a prototype platform for operational models will be constructed. The readiness level in this proposed project will advance from RL4 (validation of system in the lab or equivalent) to RL7 (demonstration in a relevant environment) over a three-year period.
Technical objectives: develop the coupling between a state-of-the-art process-based community land surface/hydrological model and a 3D community ocean circulation model to better predict temperature, salinity, water level, buoyancy, sediment, and contaminant transport through the River - Estuary - Ocean Continuum with the consideration of both barotropic and baroclinic processes.

Institutions_clean: Euro-Mediterranean Center on Climate Change (CMCC)
Public value framing:
DESYCO is a GIS-based Decision Support System (DSS) aimed at the integrated assessment of multiple climate change impacts on vulnerable coastal systems (e.g. beaches, river deltas, estuaries and lagoons, wetlands, agricultural and urban areas). It implements a Regional Risk Assessment (RRA) methodology that allows to rank potential targets and areas at risk from climate change and to identify homogeneous geographic sites for the definition of adaptation and management strategies.

DESYCO is an open source software that manages different input data (e.g. raster or shapefiles) regarding both climate change hazard scenarios (e.g. global and regional climate projections; high resolution hydrodynamic, hydrological and biogeochemical simulations) and site-specific physical, ecological and socioeconomic features of the analyzed region (e.g. coastal topography, geomorphology, presence and distribution of vegetation cover, location of artificial protection).
Technical objectives: reduce climate-related risks to coasts, wetlands, groundwater, cities, and agriculture.
Funding sources: Italian Ministry of Education, University and Research; FP7 CLIM-RUN Project (EU FP7)

Project name: Development of a dynamic systems model as a tool for planning and analyzing marine protected areas
Institutions_clean: Interdisciplinary Center for Marine Sciences (CICIMAR)
Public value framing: Protected Natural Areas (PNAs) promote conservation, mitigate climate change, generate economic benefits, and provide environmental services. In the marine environment, PNAs are often associated with reefs, which are highly diverse ecosystems. Their establishment involves a management plan that specifies clearly defined objectives, components, zones, and activities. However, this plan may change and be updated depending on how the biological, environmental, and socioeconomic components interact. In this regard, having an approach that allows for a comprehensive definition of the PNA’s structure—describing the interaction of its components—as well as tools to help predict possible outcomes under different scenarios, is essential for the decisions and actions that are implemented. The project will apply a systems analysis approach and develop a dynamic systems model using Cabo Pulmo National Park as an example.
Public values (labels): ecosystem restoration; blue economy; better decision-making
Technical objectives: The project will apply a systems analysis approach and develop a dynamic systems model using Cabo Pulmo National Park as a case study. The model will be structured with the support and guidance of specialists, key stakeholders, and local residents. It will be informed by data from scientific literature, time-series data from monitoring efforts conducted by academic institutions, civil society organizations, and government agencies, as well as the analysis of this information using indices and statistical methods. The model will be developed and simulated using the STELLA Professional software (v3.1, Isee Systems).
Notes: Information about the research project is displayed in pop-up windows.

Project name: The Digital Twin for Green Shipping (DT4GS)
Public value framing:
Analyse the application of Digital Twins in enabling decarbonisation of shipping and develop associated modelling frameworks to support practical innovations in ship performance optimisation, ship retrofitting, and Smart Green Ship new builds. Importantly, DTs provide the ability to instantaneously analyze, compare, and integrate data such as ship IoT monitoring, port state control inspections, events history terminal feedback to identify anomalies and trends.

Even though DT Product lifecycle management offers a way to long-term decarbonization in shipping, the medium-term impact is likely to be small, as world fleet renewal is at slow process. For the next 20 years, the efforts of decarbonization will primarily require investments from ship owners/operators in CO2e reducing solutions. Consequently, the target impact for DT4GS is to enable shipping companies capitalize on the DT potential to increase ship efficiencies to constantly reduce CO2e in the short-term, and to support robust investment decisions and planning [retrofit/new builds] in the longer term.
Technical objectives: develop modelling frameworks to support practical innovations in ship performance optimisation

Public value framing:
Our goal is to design, implement, and validate a pre-operational digital twin of the Greenland and Antarctic ice sheets and ice shelves (DTC-IS). By integrating Earth Observation (EO) data with advanced computational models, the aim of the DTC-IS is to fully represent the ice sheets system and address issues related to hydrology, ice shelf and ice sheet stability, ice sheet mass balance, and climatology.

The Greenland and Antarctic ice sheets are essential to the Earth system, storing vast freshwater reserves and driving sea level, ocean, and climate dynamics. Their evolution, through ice flow, surface melting, calving, and basal melting, affects ecosystems, coastal regions, and global climate. Through modular, interactive tools and stakeholder-driven use cases, the DTC-IS provides a near-real-time, science-based platform for exploring the state of the ice sheets and for performing “what-if” scenarios, supporting research, and decision-making and policies.
Public values (labels): basic research; better decision-making; stakeholder inclusion
Technical objectives: predict future impact scenarios of the ice-sheet melt

Public value framing:
The EcoScope Project aims to promote effective and efficient, ecosystem-based approach to fisheries management. EcoScope will develop an interoperable platform, a robust decision-making toolbox, a series of online courses and a mobile application. These tools will be available through a single public portal, to promote an efficient, ecosystem-based approach to the management of fisheries and achieve maximum and continuous participation of stakeholders.

The four-year (2021-2025) project addresses ecosystem degradation and anthropogenic impact that cause fisheries to be unsustainably exploited in several European Seas and promotes efficient, holistic, sustainable, ecocentric fisheries management that will aid towards restoring fisheries sustainability and ensuring balance between food security and healthy seas.
Technical objectives: The EcoScope Platform will organise and homogenise climatic, oceanographic, biogeochemical, biological and fisheries datasets for European Seas to a common standard type and format that will be available to the users through interactive mapping layers. The EcoScope Toolbox, a scoring system linked to the platform, will host ecosystem models, socio-economic indicators, fisheries and ecosystem assessment tools that can be used to examine and develop fisheries management and marine policy scenarios as well as maritime spatial planning simulations.

Public value framing: ENVRI-Hub NEXT consolidates and advances the robust conceptual and technical structure established by the ENVRI-Hub to empower the ENVRI Science Cluster to provide interdisciplinary data-driven services. These services will support climate change research, including the development of mitigation and adaptation measures and strategies, and the assessment of the climate change risks. Through this platform, ENVRI-Hub NEXT will facilitate the integration of the environmental sciences community into EOSC, guided by the concept of Essential Climate Variables.
Public values (labels): climate change adaptation; climate change mitigation
Technical objectives: As a Knowledge Base, the ENVRI-Hub provides searching capabilities to locate environmental content, including data, metadata, or notebooks, in three different ways. It offers a classical search system powered by an LLM, which provides users with a summarised response followed by a list of contents categorised into four classes: i) Web Content, ii) Metadata, iii) APIs, and iv) Jupyter Notebooks. Furthermore, we also offer a dialogue-based search system, which complements the classical search with a dialogue-based style of natural communication, allowing users to discuss and analyse content with follow-ups. Finally, we provide a virtual research environment to run the shared notebooks, which we found from the search system to explore the datasets, metadata, and APIs. Overall, to consolidate all the services, a federated authentication system is implemented that enables RIs to easily and seamlessly index their data while adhering to FAIR principles.

Public value framing: ENVRINNOV will: i) Conduct a comprehensive analysis of ENVRI services and technological needs & gaps, and define how to monitor them regularly. ii) Define, digitalize, and promote the uptake of common ENVRI innovation strategies for new technologies/services development to meet emerging needs/gaps. This will be done by defining and testing innovation co-creation mechanisms between ENVRIs, industry and the scientific community and shaping them into common policies. The project will also develop an ENVRI innovation capacity-building programme, a digital platform to enable them, and an uptake strategy to promote them. iii) Engage the European Strategy Forum on Research Infrastructures (ESFRI) and R&I environment ecosystems to ensure long-term synergies and complementarities with ENVRI and gather feedback and support for the Roadmap.
Technical objectives:
ENVRINNOV’s approach supports ESFRI’s strategic objective to “accelerate the exploitation of EU RIs as knowledge & innovation hubs”. It will help strengthen the European response to the climate crisis and its associated scientific, societal and economic challenges, by increasing the capacity of ENVRIs to respond to them. It will contribute to a more effective EU RI landscape, and support better integration across thematic areas and with the EU Technology Infrastructure landscape.

ENVRINNOV’s impact will manifest on multiple levels , unlocking opportunities for novel environmental monitoring technology solutions and data applications, while indirectly promoting the development of the Technology Infrastructure dimension in various RIs.

Institutions_clean: Ericsson
Public value framing: The overall port pilot is expected to bring significant benefits to the port. This includes: Reduction of the registration phase from 3 minutes to 2 minutes; Reduction in the average execution time of moving an object from A to B with a forklift from 8 minutes to 7 minutes; Reduction in the space occupied in the storage phase by 10 percent, for example from 5000sqm to 4500sqm; Reduction in the unloading/loading times of a general cargo ship from an average of 18 hours to 16 hours, meaning the relevant idle time at berth is reduced from 36 hours to 34 hours; For the latter, the time saving can be immediately translated into significant cost saving for the ship’s owners. In addition, more ships could be served through the course of the year, scaling up the port’s capacity without the need to enlarge the existing terminals. All these efficiency gains also result in a reduction in movements during cargo handling. This can significantly optimize the overall process, lowering fuel consumption as well as associated CO2 emissions by 8.2 percent
Technical objectives: capture of massive data from smart sensors, 3D LIDARs, and Wide Dynamic Range (WDR) cameras in the area; having these data available in real-time, an operation control system based on  (AI), developed and deployed by Ericsson for the project, determines the sequence of logistics tasks and activities, by correlating a real-time information flow captured from smart sensors, from cameras, and from vehicles operating in the area. Tasks include the movement, tracking and positioning of forklifts and freights, as well as the inventories of goods, orders of loading and unloading of ships. This could also mean identifying specific activities, like using cranes and moving larger loads, as with a “high risk” flagging. The detailed view of everything happening at the port allows the AI engine to provide feedback on processes and share live updates with port supervisors.

Public value framing: the overall objective of FAIR-EASE is to customize and operate distributed and integrated services for observation and modelling of the Earth system, environment and biodiversity by improving their different components implemented in close cooperation with user-communities, the European Open Science Cloud (EOSC) and research infrastructures in their design and sustainable availability.
Technical objectives: FAIR-EASE focuses on opening these gateways for the earth and environmental sciences which require a large volume of data from sources such as satellite, in-situ observations, models, and even omics experiments. Earth system domains are interconnected, but the present digital architecture is based essentially on distributed and domain-dependent data repositories inducing real difficulties for integrated uses of all the environmental data. This creates barriers for several studies with large societal impacts, such as climate change, agriculture and food, human safety and health.

Public value framing:
The main goal of the project is to increase the profits of fishermen by commercializing the results of the research-implementation project (accomplished by “first production”) implemented by R&D institutions and enterprises. The subject of the project is the construction of the Knowledge Transfer Platform – FindFISH based on in-situ research, environmental data and quantitative and qualitative fishing data as well as numerical modelling of hydrodynamic, physicochemical and biological parameters of the Gulf of Gdansk. The FindFISH platform is a database containing historical data and current, online forecasts on the marine environment and living resources of the Gulf of Gdansk. It was created by means of knowledge transfer between two groups of users: scientists and fishermen. The FindFISH platform provides ongoing forecasts about the conditions of the marine environment in the Gulf of Gdańsk. It uses an operational model. The model is created based on in situ data obtained during fishing trips and transferred by fishermen as well as numerical data provided by scientists. The forecasts will show under what hydrological conditions industrial fisheries for four species - herring, sprat, cod, and flounder - should be the most abundant in resources.

FindFISH is a service built for ZRM-OP that will make it available to a wide range of interested parties.
Technical objectives: Creation of knowledge transfer platform
Funding sources: Pomorskie Voivodeship Regional Operational Programme for 2014-2020

Project name: Fluid dynamics in rotation with topography.
Institutions_clean: Ensenada Center for Scientific Research and Advanced Studies (CICESE)
Public value framing: The main objectives are focused on gaining a better understanding of the evolution of oceanic and atmospheric vortices, which are characterized by their dependence on the Earth’s rotation and, under certain circumstances, on the topography of the seafloor. Examples of such structures include tropical cyclones in the atmosphere and warm- and cold-water eddies in the ocean, which can be influenced by continental landforms (atmosphere) or underwater mountain ranges and canyons (ocean). An example of interest to our country is the interaction of anticyclonic eddies (warm water) with the continental shelf off the coast of Tamaulipas in the Gulf of Mexico. In this case, it is important to investigate whether the eddies maintain their structure or not, and how they are distributed along the coast. In the atmosphere, on the other hand, there is evidence suggesting the influence of the Sierra Madre on the drift of tropical cyclones formed either in the Pacific Ocean or in the Gulf of Mexico. Apparently, the vortices tend to be deflected toward the Sierra, moving closer to the continent, due to topographic effects induced by the Sierra’s presence and orientation. This example demonstrates the significance that topographic effects can have on vortices as distant as cyclones formed over the ocean.
Technical objectives: to study oceanic and atmospheric vortices using experimental models

Project name: Integrated Valuation of Ecosystem Services and Trade-offs (InVEST)
Institutions_clean: The Natural Capital Project
Public value framing:
InVEST® is a suite of free, open-source software models used to map and value the goods and services from nature that sustain and fulfill human life.  If properly managed, ecosystems yield a flow of services that are vital to humanity, including the production of goods (e.g., food), life-support processes (e.g., water purification), and life-fulfilling conditions (e.g., beauty, opportunities for recreation), and the conservation of options (e.g., genetic diversity for future use).  Despite its importance, this natural capital is poorly understood, scarcely monitored, and, in many cases, undergoing rapid degradation and depletion.

Governments, non-profits, international lending institutions, and corporations all manage natural resources for multiple uses and inevitably must evaluate tradeoffs among them. The multi-service, modular design of InVEST provides an effective tool for balancing the environmental and economic goals of these diverse entities.

InVEST enables decision makers to assess quantified tradeoffs associated with alternative management choices and to identify areas where investment in natural capital can enhance human development and conservation.  The toolset includes distinct ecosystem service models designed for terrestrial, freshwater, marine, and coastal ecosystems, as well as a number of “helper tools” to assist with locating and processing input data and with understanding and visualizing outputs.
Technical objectives: The toolset includes distinct ecosystem service models designed for terrestrial, freshwater, marine, and coastal ecosystems, as well as a number of “helper tools” to assist with locating and processing input data and with understanding and visualizing outputs.

It turns out that we don’t really understand their behavior and struggle to predict when jellyfish swarms will appear, how long they’ll stay along our shores, or which species of jellyfish are in the sea. Given this lack of knowledge, we decided to launch a website in Hebrew to help you—beachgoers and beach workers alike—gather information about jellyfish along Israel’s coastline. Our project, on the other hand utilizes a unique approach and characterizes future conditions. Once the user selects a location of interest, sea-level rise scenario (e.g., Intermediate High) and target year (e.g., 2050), the tool will provide plan-view and cross-sectional information specific to their selection including upland topography (e.g., DEM, elevation profile and slope) and scenario specific future conditions (e.g., shoreline location, parcel ownership, land use, and demographic data). Public value framing: The overall aim of MARBEFES is to establish clear links between biodiversity, ecosystem functions, and the societal benefits they provide. This is achieved through an innovative suite of ecological, economic, and socio-cultural tools, enabling a comprehensive valuation that supports sustainable policy and governance for marine ecosystems. MARBEFES seeks to go beyond current knowledge by analyzing the causes and consequences of biodiversity changes and assessing how these affect the ecological and economic value of European seas.
Technical objectives: MARBEFES explicitly considers the river-to-ocean gradient, utilizing 12 Broad Belt Transects (BBTs) across the four main marine regions of the EU – the Arctic, Baltic, Atlantic, and Mediterranean. These transects serve as the core of MARBEFES research, providing data to understand biodiversity trends and their impacts across Europe.

Public value framing: Global marine ecosystems, including the RMG and the broader Eastern Tropical Pacific (ETP) region, are facing degradation due to a steady increase in anthropogenic pressures. Climate change, marine invasions, pollution, growing tourism, and more intensive fishing activities are among the documented threats, which jeopardize both people’s livelihoods and the sustainability and overall function of these unique ecosystems. Our work seeks to assess the effects of introduced invasive species, climate change, and other anthropogenic pressures on biodiversity in the Galápagos Marine Reserve and the Eastern Tropical Pacific, with the aim of better supporting coastal communities and preventing the extinction of marine species.
Public values (labels): mitigate biodiversity loss; climate change adaptation; coastal zone protection
Technical objectives: To study underwater biodiversity and assess human impacts in order to develop effective diagnostic indicators for better management and adaptation to climate variability. Our researchers use climate models to assess changes in ocean currents caused by rising temperatures and to determine how these changes affect the transport of nutrients, larvae, and non-native species. This information is essential for understanding climate change patterns and helping us predict the ecological responses of the entire ecosystem.
Funding sources: Blue Action Fund; Paul M. Angell Family Foundation; Re:wild; Galapagos Conservation Trust; Ken Collins; Amy Blackwell; The Rohr Foundation; Oceans Finance Company; Galapagos Conservancy; Lindblad Expeditions - National Geographic Fund
Geographic coverage: Ecuador (Galápagos Marine Reserve and the Tropical Eastern Pacific)

Project name: Monitoring of Oil Spills in the Southern Gulf of Mexico
Institutions_clean: Interdisciplinary Center for Marine Sciences (CICIMAR)
Public value framing: Marine pollution caused by oil spills is a serious global environmental problem due to its environmental, social, and economic impacts. The southern Gulf of Mexico is a region particularly vulnerable to spill pollution because of the oil extraction and transportation activities carried out there. In addition to the major spills caused by accidents at the Ixtoc-1 (1979) and Deepwater Horizon (2010) platforms, numerous spills have recently been reported in areas near the oil infrastructure of the Cantarell Complex. This research protocol proposes implementing a continuous monitoring system to detect oil spills in the southern Gulf of Mexico, as well as the extent of these spills and their possible trajectories.
Technical objectives: improve the response time of mitigation measures to reduce the environmental impact caused by [oil spills] on aquatic ecosystems and coastal areas.

Public value framing: All these aspects require a solid scientific foundation for observing, understanding, and modeling the marine environment. The anticipated implications of climate change and human impact in the Mediterranean Sea fully justify the continuation of holistic, multidisciplinary observation by the coastal states, with the long-term goal of establishing a European strategy for Mediterranean observation. In this context, a regional-scale multidisciplinary and multi-platform observation system, MOOSE, was established in 2010 as an integrated regional network covering the northwestern Mediterranean coast. Its objective is to observe and identify long-term environmental anomalies, taking into account seasonal and interannual variations, as well as the impact of extreme events on physical and biogeochemical processes affecting marine biodiversity. It relies on a network of autonomous sites and platforms that cover various temporal and spatial scales essential for understanding the processes affecting the water column, from the surface to the depths, as well as the interactions between coastal and offshore zones, and between the ocean, the atmosphere, and rivers.
Technical objectives: using proven techniques and procedures to ensure homogeneity of observation at all sites, encourage shared analyses and develop common collection and analysis protocols (good practice)
Funding sources: French Ministry of Higher Education through CNRS/INSU
Geographic coverage: Mediterranean basin, from the continental shelf (coastal observatories) to the open sea

Project name: Nucleus for European Modelling of the Ocean
Institutions_clean: Nucleus for European Modelling of the Ocean (NEMO)
Public value framing:
NEMO is an open-source codebase used for ocean dynamics, biogeochemistry and sea-ice modelling
applications, including research, forecasts and climate projections. It is managed by a consortium of
institutes that support and develop NEMO for their own purposes and the wider community.
The Nucleus for European Modelling of the Ocean (NEMO1) is a geoscientific model (the NEMO
codebase), used for a variety of applications covering research on ocean and sea-ice dynamics,
operational forecasts (short-range, seasonal and decadal), re-analyses, climate projections and the
preparation of ocean observing systems. The development of the NEMO codebase is supported by a
group of institutions (the NEMO consortium) who pool resources to develop the codebase in a
sustainable way. This undertaking serves not only the needs of the NEMO consortium institutions but
also those of a broader group of users and interested parties (the NEMO community). The NEMO
development strategy is intended to define priorities for developing the NEMO codebase to the
benefit of the NEMO consortium and of the broader NEMO community.
Public values (labels): basic research; stakeholder inclusion; better decision-making
Technical objectives:
NEMO is an open source geoscientific model that can be used to numerically
represents the ocean and sea-ice from the global scale to sub-kilometre-scale. The NEMO codebase
consists of three main components: the ocean circulation component NEMO-OCE; the sea-ice
dynamics and thermodynamics component NEMO-SI3; the tracer transport component with an
interface for ocean biogeochemistry NEMO-TOP and the biogeochemistry component NEMO-PISCES.
These physical components are described in their respective reference manuals, available on zenodo.
The NEMO codebase comes with a series of Reference configurations and test cases, for users to set
up new applications, and for developers to test and validate new functionalities. It The NEMO
codebase also includes additional capabilities such as an IO server, a two-way nesting package, a
coupling interface, and pre- and post-processing tools2. The NEMO codebase is freely available under
the public CeCILL licence, with official release versions issued every few years. The code development
is hosted openly on a Gitlab server3 and the code is distributed with a list of reference configurations
with their input and set-up files.
Data collection methods: remote sensing; in-situ; desk research; citizen science

Institutions_clean: Los Alamos National Laboratory
Public value framing: Since the CESM1.2 release, there have been improvements to both the physical parameterizations and numerical methods in the CESM ocean component, Parallel Ocean Program version 2 (POP2). The physics improvements include a new parameterization for mixing effects in estuaries to improve the representation of the exchange of freshwater between the terrestrial and marine branches of the hydrologic cycle; increased mesoscale eddy diffusivities at depth to improve the representation of passive tracers; use of prognostic chlorophyll for short-wave absorption; use of salinity dependent freezing-point together with the sea-ice model; and a new Langmuir mixing parameterization in conjunction with the new wave model component. The numerical improvements include a new iterative solver for the barotropic mode to reduce communication costs, particularly advantageous for high-resolution simulations on large processor counts; a new time filtering scheme based on an adaption of the Robert filter to enable sub-diurnal coupling of the ocean model; and subsequently, use of one-hour coupling frequency to explicitly resolve (sub-)diurnal and inertial periods. In addition, the K-Profile vertical mixing Parameterization (KPP) is incorporated via the Community ocean Vertical Mixing (CVMix) framework and the Caspian Sea is no longer included in the ocean model as a marginal sea. Finally, ocean biogeochemisty has been modularized under the Marine Biogeochemistry Library (MARBL) to enable portability to alternative physical frameworks.
Technical objectives: A three-dimensional ocean circulation model designed primarily for studying the ocean climate system. The model is developed and supported primarily by researchers at LANL.

Institutions_clean: Federal University of Rio Grande do Sul
Head_institutions: Federal University of Rio Grande do Sul
Public value framing: The geomorphological map serves as an inventory of substrate characteristics and processes. The objectives of this study were to refine the existing geomorphological mapping and to produce a new geomorphological map of the southern coastal zone of the state of Sergipe using remote sensing techniques. The methodology included digital image processing, digital elevation modeling, fieldwork, granulometric and statistical analysis of sediments, and the creation of the geomorphological map. The southern coastal zone of Sergipe comprises two geomorphological units: coastal terraces and the coastal plain, which are connected by inactive cliffs. The coastal plain was divided into seven subunits: inner marine terraces, paleodunes, wetlands/lagoons, outer marine terraces, tidal flats/mangroves, current dunes/interdunes, and beaches. The units/subunits exhibited distinct spectral responses, particularly regarding color and relief (texture), in digital image processing and the digital elevation model. In digital image processing, it was possible to distinguish between: vegetation types; exposed sandy substrate due to the spectral behavior of quartz sands; “clear water” and “water with suspended sediment”; and alignments and truncations of coastal ridges. The digital elevation model highlighted units/subunits with topographic differences, such as coastal flats, paleodunes, and current dunes. This study underscored the importance of remote sensing for improving the geomorphological mapping of coastal zones.
Technical objectives: reveal the importance of remote sensing to better map geomorphological coasts

Project name: Potential Impact of Climate Change on Scaled Fish Fisheries in Baja California Sur
Institutions_clean: Interdisciplinary Center for Marine Sciences (CICIMAR)
Public value framing: The climate scenarios presented in the Sixth Assessment Report of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change indicate that Mexico’s coastal areas will be threatened by rising sea levels and increased storm and hurricane intensity, as well as rising temperatures and ocean acidification—all of which occur alongside declines in oxygen concentrations and primary productivity. This outlook highlights that climate change must become a priority issue for decision-makers, communities, and other stakeholders concerned with the well-being of the fisheries sector, in order to promote the adoption of management measures from an adaptive perspective and within a framework of participatory governance.
Public values (labels): climate change adaptation; blue economy; stakeholder inclusion
Technical objectives: To understand the potential impact of climate change on fisheries, we propose developing models that predict changes in the distribution of fish stocks due to the environmental changes currently affecting the oceans. Once local-scale distribution models have been developed, we will assess the extent of change by comparing current distribution patterns with those suggested by the models under various scenarios. In addition, the relative importance index (RII) for the ten species of interest will be estimated by fishing zone/office; the impact of climate change will depend on the RII value and changes in distribution.

Institutions_clean: Ensenada Center for Scientific Research and Advanced Studies (CICESE)
Public value framing: Currently, socioeconomically significant issues, such as oil spills in the ocean or the arrival of sargassum on Mexican shores, have highlighted the need for ocean prediction models equipped with data assimilation systems. Furthermore, the scientific community has found these numerical models to be powerful tools that enable us to answer questions of current relevance.
Technical objectives: This project brings together three areas of research: (i) systems for data assimilation and ocean forecasting, (ii) coupled physical-biogeochemical models, and (iii) submesoscale and vertical mixing processes. Through these lines of research, the aim is to improve our understanding of the ocean’s physical-biogeochemical system, while also proposing a forecasting system based on the assimilation of operational observations.

Institutions_clean: Ensenada Center for Scientific Research and Advanced Studies (CICESE)
Public value framing: Studying the primary modes of variability shared between the ocean and the atmosphere helps us better understand the influence and interdependence between these two systems, and how this interaction changes over time. Understanding how the ocean-atmosphere system covaries is important for improving simulation and prediction models of the ocean, atmosphere, and climate, as well as for providing environmental context for research and management of marine ecosystems and fisheries.
Technical objectives: to identify ocean-atmosphere variability modes in different regions of the Mexican Pacific, how these modes are linked to regional and global climate changes, and how they relate to changes in the coastal zone. Of particular interest are recent variability trends and the identification of regions that require further analysis to understand their current conditions.
Notes: Information about the research project is displayed in pop-up windows.

Project name: Support for Research on the Physical Mechanisms of Climate Variations in Mexico
Institutions_clean: Ensenada Center for Scientific Research and Advanced Studies (CICESE)
Public value framing: The climate of the region encompassing Mexico, the United States, and Central America has undergone significant changes in recent decades. For example, rapid warming has been observed, consistent with the current global trend, along with a widespread increase in the frequency, duration, and intensity of extreme events such as droughts and atmospheric and oceanic heat waves. This has severe impacts on the population, water resources, and the local economy (...) Understanding the processes that drive climate variability in Mexico is a critical issue given the rapid climate change the region is experiencing.
Technical objectives: General Objective: To contribute to the understanding of the physical mechanisms that modulate natural and anthropogenic climate variations in Mexico, the United States, and Central America. Specific Objectives: 1. To generate recent scenarios and medium-term future projections for composite events involving droughts and atmospheric heat waves in Mexico using available global simulations. 2. Implement a high-resolution coupled ocean-atmosphere model (NEMO-OASIS-WRF)
Geographic coverage: Mexico; the United States; Central America
Notes: Information about the research project is displayed in pop-up windows.

Project name: Support for the fundamentals of numerical wave prediction and simulation, as well as ocean-atmosphere exchange
Institutions_clean: Ensenada Center for Scientific Research and Advanced Studies (CICESE)
Public value framing: Gaining a detailed understanding of wave phenomena and being able to accurately predict them in coastal regions is one of the most pressing issues for a variety of applications. In particular, the management and prediction of beach erosion, the estimation of mixing in coastal areas, as well as safety in navigation and other maritime activities, stand to benefit from the results of this project.
Technical objectives: Use advanced models that attempt to describe the evolution of the wave spectrum in the coastal region, based on numerically implemented source functions that represent the processes of energy supply and dissipation, as well as its redistribution due to nonlinear interactions among the spectral components.
Notes: Information about the research project is displayed in pop-up windows.

Head_institutions: The National Geological Surveys for Denmark and Greenland (GEUS)
Public value framing:
HIP provides open public hydrological data, model calculations, and future forecasts to support climate adaptation, water management, and planning efforts.
HIP provides consolidated access to freely available public data on surface-level hydrological conditions to support climate adaptation, water management, emergency preparedness activities, and other planning where water plays a role. In the coming years, billions will be invested in climate adaptation, and it is important to have a solid data foundation for making sound and sustainable decisions.
HIP is an open, web-based user interface that displays data and model calculations regarding near-surface hydrological conditions.  Visualization of data in HIP is intended to support collaboration and dialogue across professional groups and between authorities, businesses, and citizens to address challenges related to too little or too much water. HIP data creates value for municipal planners and is relevant for staff working with watercourses, wastewater, and groundwater. Consolidated access to measurements and hydrological model calculations will also support business innovation and help create new opportunities for climate adaptation. he method development project confirmed the great value of data provided by municipalities, water utilities, regions, and the state to obtain the best possible model calculations. 
 Municipalities have the option to report cross-section data for watercourses, so that this data can be viewed and downloaded from HIP for use in flood mapping and other analyses.
Public values (labels): climate change adaptation; disaster prevention; basic research; stakeholder inclusion
Technical objectives: simulates the  hydrological cycle for the entire country
